text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic cholecystectomy by taking down the omentum, revealing markers of previous inflammation around the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears not actively inflamed, allowing for clear identification of its wall, neck, and infundibulum.

The surgeon applies upper traction to achieve self-retaining retraction, bringing the omentum and duodenum down. The hook cautery shows thermal spread towards the duodenum, prompting the use of cold scissors to minimize further thermal spread. The gallbladder is mobilized, revealing its bottom, and the surgeon begins dissecting up the right side by opening the peritoneum for improved retraction and manipulation.

The surgeon dissects the left side of the gallbladder, carefully avoiding the artery by lateralizing it away from the gallbladder wall. The peritoneum is opened directly over the artery, heading towards the liver, while continuing to run up the left side, similar to the previous dissection on the right side.

The surgeon further mobilizes the artery, carefully separating it from the cystic duct. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to enhance visualization and ensure no aberrant vessels are present. The artery is traced up onto the gallbladder, draping it back towards the Calot's triangle to achieve the critical view.

The surgeon uses ICG mode to identify the common duct and confirm the presence of a long cystic duct without branching. The lateral mobilization on the right side of the gallbladder aids in cleaning off the back of the cystic duct, ensuring a clear view before proceeding with dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates the achieved critical view of safety, showing clear identification of the cystic duct and artery. The artery and duct are clipped, followed by division. The gallbladder is then carefully dissected from the liver bed, staying within the adventitial plane.
The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder, taking a final look at the surgical site. The bifurcation of the hepatic ducts is clearly visible in the field.
